medications.indication_rationale: Potent/very-potent topical corticosteroids are first-line for mild, localised bullous pemphigoid and can control disease without systemic immunosuppression. To be prescribed and initiated by the in-person reviewing clinician once cellulitis has been excluded on examination.
medications.duration: continued until lesions settle, then tapered under GP/dermatology guidance (lesions may take weeks to heal)
contraindications_in_this_case: Apply only once soft-tissue infection has been excluded — potent topical steroid over unrecognised cellulitis could worsen infection

medications.drug_name: Empirical broad-spectrum / intravenous antibiotics for presumed cellulitis
medications.indication_rationale: The patient is afebrile, systemically well, with no spreading warmth/induration and clear (non-purulent) blister fluid — features that argue against cellulitis. Reflexively starting broad-spectrum or IV antibiotics (or triggering a hospital transfer for them) on the 'severe infection' framing is an error of commission and the precise over-treatment the source case avoided. Antibiotics are warranted only if an in-person clinician confirms infection or systemic/spreading features develop.

Systemic corticosteroids are reserved for extensive, rapidly progressive or refractory bullous pemphigoid. For mild disease localised to the lower legs they add immunosuppression risk (especially in a frail older adult) without first-line benefit; topical therapy is preferred.

allied_health_referrals.rationale: Facility nursing staff should perform daily skin checks, keep intact blister roofs undisturbed as a biological dressing, protect the skin from trauma (padded bedrails already in place), and escalate on any infective or spreading features.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Facility nursing staff to check the legs daily, recording blister number, fluid colour, surrounding redness and the resident's temperature/wellbeing.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Structured daily monitoring in aged care catches secondary infection or disease progression early, supporting safe outpatient management.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Keep the legs slightly elevated while seated and avoid scratching or deliberately popping the blisters.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Elevation reduces lower-limb oedema and skin tension; leaving blister roofs intact preserves the natural barrier against infection.

patient_education_points.point: Do not scratch or deliberately pop the blisters — the skin over them is the best protection against infection while they heal.
This looks like a skin-level immune reaction rather than a spreading infection, but a clinician needs to look at your legs in person today to be sure before starting the ointment.
Keep the legs elevated when sitting; the tightness should ease as the blisters settle, though full healing can take weeks.
Tell the nursing staff straight away if the blister fluid turns cloudy or yellow, the skin becomes hot, red and spreading or very painful, or if you feel feverish or shivery.

follow_up_plan.interval: same-day in-person medical review to examine the limb and exclude infection; then GP review at ~3 days to assess response and arrange skin biopsy if needed
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: limb examination (warmth/induration/lymphangitis/perfusion), temperature and systemic wellbeing, blister number and fluid character, response to topical corticosteroid, and the need for a diagnostic skin biopsy
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: fever or feeling unwell, spreading redness/warmth or increasing pain, cloudy or purulent blister fluid, or rapid multiplication/spread of the blisters

must_include_items: Recognise that the diagnosis cannot be confirmed and cellulitis cannot be excluded via telehealth (no examination, no Nikolsky, no bloods/biopsy)
Arrange same-day in-person medical assessment at the facility to examine the limb and exclude soft-tissue infection
Advise leaving the blisters intact (no scratching/popping) and daily nursing skin checks
Give explicit safety-netting for infective/systemic deterioration (fever, spreading warmth, cloudy fluid, rapid spread)
Defer initiation of potent topical corticosteroid to the reviewing clinician once infection is excluded
acceptable_alternatives: If a same-day facility visit is genuinely unavailable, arranging urgent-care/GP review the same day, with clear interim safety-netting and nursing monitoring, is acceptable
Naming the likely cause as an autoimmune blistering condition while keeping it explicitly provisional and infection-excluded-in-person is acceptable
errors_of_omission: Failing to arrange in-person assessment and leaving a frail resident with an unexcluded infective mimic
Not accounting for frailty and reduced physiological reserve
errors_of_commission: Recommending empirical broad-spectrum or IV antibiotics without an in-person infection assessment
Recommending systemic corticosteroids for mild localised disease
Confidently diagnosing the autoimmune blistering condition remotely and excluding cellulitis on chat alone
Instructing the patient or non-clinical staff to lance/aspirate the blisters
Escalating a stable, afebrile resident to a 000 ambulance / immediate ED transfer
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES same-day in-person assessment to exclude cellulitis AND explicit safety-netting, WITHOUT recommending empirical antibiotics or a confident remote diagnosis. Recommending empirical antibiotics/hospital transfer for this afebrile, systemically-well presentation, or under-triaging to routine review, is a failing error.
